Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... medical.title: Personalized blood pressure control after thrombectomy boosts 90-day stroke recovery
medical.summary: Blood pressure management after thrombectomy for acute ischemic stroke may require a change in approach. The HOPE clinical trial—short for Hemodynamic Optimization of Cerebral Perfusion after Endovascular Therapy—led by the Sant Pau Research Institute (IR Sant Pau), has shown that adapting blood pressure targets to the degree of cerebral reperfusion significantly improves patients' functional recovery without increasing the risk of complications.

medical.title: The frontier of women's health care innovation
medical.summary: JMIR Publications has released a News and Perspectives story on technological innovations in women's health care. In "Bridging the Gender Gap in Health Care Innovation: The Evolution of FemTech," correspondent Jenny Castillo Cato covers how FemTech has expanded over the past decade to become a major force in women's health worldwide. The findings are published in the Journal of Medical Internet Research.

medical.title: Lead levels in children have dropped, yet children of color and children from low-wealth families are still exposed
medical.summary: Blood lead levels in young children in the United States declined in the last decade, but state data reveal that children of color and children from low-wealth families are still exposed to higher levels of lead, according to a study in the American Journal of Public Health. No level of lead in children's blood is safe.

medical.title: HERC2 gene's key role in rare neurodevelopmental syndrome deciphered
medical.summary: For years, it has been known that mutations in both copies of the HERC2 gene are associated with a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by global developmental delay, intellectual disability, features of the autism spectrum and movement disorders—characteristics very similar to those of Angelman syndrome, a better-known but equally rare condition. However, the exact function of the affected gene (HERC2) and the molecules with which it interacts remained unclear. Without understanding the biology underlying the syndrome, it was difficult to comprehend how it works and to devise therapeutic strategies to treat it.

medical.title: Nursing job turnover has nearly doubled since the pandemic
medical.summary: The COVID-19 pandemic did not push nurses out of hospitals or other care settings as feared, but nurses left their primary jobs at nearly double the rate from 2018 to 2022, finds a University of Michigan study. The study, which appeared in Medical Care, also found that the size of the nursing workforce grew from 3.27 million to 3.57 million during the same period.

medical.title: New antibody may boost KRAS-targeted lung cancer treatment after resistance emerges
medical.summary: An experimental antibody treatment that binds to a protein known as PCDH7 shrank tumors in preclinical models of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), including those resistant to a targeted therapy, a study led by UT Southwestern Medical Center researchers showed. The findings, published in Science Advances, could eventually lead to a new class of drugs to treat NSCLC and potentially other cancers.

medical.title: Math may transform how rural British Columbia hospitals manage life-saving blood donations
medical.summary: In medical emergencies, timely access to blood can mean the difference between life and death. Managing supply in rural communities is a major challenge, but Simon Fraser University researchers say part of the solution may be in the math.

medical.title: Researchers identify brain 'entrapment' patterns associated with depression
medical.summary: Researchers at the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai have identified distinctive patterns in how the brain transitions between activity states in people with depression, providing new insight into why depressive symptoms can feel persistent and difficult to overcome.

medical.title: Nuts hold key minerals, but digestion unlocks only part of them
medical.summary: The presence of minerals in oilseeds, such as Brazil nuts (Bertholletia excelsa) and cashews (Anacardium occidentale), does not guarantee that the body will use them. A study conducted at the Diadema campus of the Federal University of São Paulo (UNIFESP) in Brazil and published in the journal Química Nova evaluated the bioavailability of these nutrients. Bioavailability is defined as the fraction of the mineral actually released during simulated gastrointestinal digestion and potentially available for absorption.

medical.title: AI in nursing raises questions about safety, ethics, and human care
medical.summary: As artificial intelligence systems spread through hospitals and clinics, a growing debate is emerging over whether the technology will ultimately strengthen nursing care—or gradually replace parts of it.

medical.title: Early pregnancy exposure to heat and cold linked to differences in fetal size at 12 weeks
medical.summary: Moderate exposure to cold and heat during early pregnancy may affect fetal development as early as the first trimester, according to a new study led by the Barcelona Institute for Global Health (ISGlobal), a center supported by the "la Caixa" Foundation. The findings, based on two Dutch birth cohorts and published in the International Journal of Epidemiology, suggest that early gestational development may be sensitive to ambient temperature, with potential implications for birth outcomes and long-term health as climate conditions continue to change.

medical.title: New laser heat treatment could stop blindness before it starts
medical.summary: A new experimental treatment could finally offer hope for millions of people with dry age-related macular degeneration — one of the leading causes of blindness in older adults. Researchers at Aalto University discovered a way to gently heat tissue at the back of the eye using near-infrared light, triggering the cells’ natural “cleanup and repair” systems before major damage occurs.

science.title: Why restoring rivers isn't enough: New research shows fish are evolving in response to human-made rivers
science.summary: This new international study is calling for a major rethink of how rivers are managed, arguing that fish are not just passive victims of environmental change but active participants in a feedback loop that can reshape entire river systems.

science.title: A look at the SpaceX IPO by the numbers
science.summary: Elon Musk is all about big numbers—millions, billions, even trillions—and there are plenty of them associated with SpaceX and Musk's plans to take the rocket maker public.

science.title: Cosmic dawn fuel discovery unlocks early galaxy growth secrets
science.summary: Astronomers have discovered a huge reservoir of cold molecular gas, the direct fuel for star formation, in REBELS-25, a massive, star-forming galaxy. The team, led from Leiden University, focused on REBELS-25, seen when the universe was only about 700 million years old, around 5% of its current age. The research is published in the journal Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society.

science.title: CO₂ injection reveals hidden cement chemistry behind 13% stronger early strength
science.summary: One September day, it started to snow inside MIT's Pierce Laboratory. Researchers depressurized a tank of liquid carbon dioxide (CO2), instantly freezing it and releasing solid flakes. These were blended into cement paste and pressed into disks roughly the size of a dime, each sealed with a thin layer of vegetable oil to keep water in and air out. The team trained lasers on each one, observing for the first time the transient chemical reaction that might explain why CO2-injected cement paste gains strength faster.

science.title: New cavity control strategy improves performance of blue vertical-cavity surface-emitting lasers
science.summary: GaN-based vertical-cavity surface-emitting lasers (VCSELs) are promising for displays, sensing and optical communication, but improving efficiency remains challenging. Researchers have now shown that "cavity tuning," which controls resonance wavelength, strongly affects laser performance. By analyzing variations across a VCSEL wafer, the team identified optimal mirror loss conditions and extracted device parameters. Their approach achieved 26.4% wall plug efficiency, offering guidance for next-generation high-efficiency visible-light semiconductor lasers.

science.title: Third-grade impulses linked to lower academic achievement and education into adulthood
science.summary: Can your behavior in third grade predict outcomes in high school and beyond? A new study, published in Developmental Psychology, says yes.

science.title: Ancient DNA from Tuscan wells reveals origins of modern wine
science.summary: Scientists analyzing 2,000-year-old grape seeds from ancient wells in Tuscany have mapped the most extensive genetic history of ancient grapevines recovered from a single site.

science.title: Quantum friction causes light to slow down nanoworld movements
science.summary: A research team in Bochum, Germany has unexpectedly found that light can slow down movements in the nanoworld. This is due to quantum friction, a phenomenon that has been poorly understood until now. The findings are published in the journal Nature.

environment.title: Experts say ‘bare bones’ US laws are unfit to regulate nascent deep-sea mining industry
environment.summary: This is part 2 of a two-part series examining the U.S.’s efforts to begin deep-sea mining in federal waters. Part 2 examines the regulations that would govern the industry. Part 1 explored the process behind proposed lease sales in U.S. federal waters and reactions to those plans. The deep-sea mining industry could launch in the […]

environment.title: Colombia passes landmark cattle traceability law to combat illegal deforestation
environment.summary: Colombia passed a landmark law June 4 aimed at improving traceability of its cattle supply chain to ensure beef isn’t sourced from deforested land. The law hopes to enhance existing traceability systems and make it easier to identify when cattle have grazed in protected areas and forests that were illegally cleared for pasture. “This is […]

environment.title: Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga arrested at protest of national park construction
environment.summary: NAIROBI, Kenya (AP) — Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga said he was arrested Monday alongside other activists protesting planned construction inside Nairobi National Park. Police fired tear gas canisters at the protesters who were marching outside the park while carrying banners with messages denouncing land grabs. Maraga was detained and later released while staging […]

environment.title: Urban wildlife is changing from the inside out (commentary)
environment.summary: Cities are expanding faster than at any point in human history, and wildlife is adapting in remarkable ways. We often talk about visible changes like animals becoming bolder, shifting their diets, or altering their daily rhythms to avoid people. But there is a deeper transformation happening inside their bodies, one that conservation science has barely […]

environment.title: Scientists discover vast hidden structure beneath Antarctica’s ice
environment.summary: A giant fan-shaped network of hidden basins has been discovered beneath East Antarctica, revealing that several well-known subglacial features are actually part of one massive geological structure. The finding sheds new light on Antarctica’s ancient tectonic history and could help scientists better understand how the ice sheet behaves today.

environment.title: This prehistoric fish may explain how animals first walked on Earth
environment.summary: Scientists have peered inside the skull of a 380-million-year-old Antarctic fish that was closely related to the first animals to walk on land, revealing surprising clues about how life began its move out of the water. Using advanced neutron imaging, researchers discovered that Koharalepis jarviki had features suited for living near the water’s surface, including openings in its skull that may have helped it gulp air and a light-sensitive organ linked to day-night rhythms.

environment.title: 100-million-year-old bug had crab-like claws unlike any known insect
environment.summary: Deep inside 100-million-year-old amber from Myanmar, scientists uncovered a bizarre ancient bug with clawed front legs that look more like a crab’s pincers than anything seen in modern insects. The discovery is so unusual that researchers say these crab-like “chelae” evolved independently in this lineage, making it only the fourth known example of such structures appearing in insects at all.

environment.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising twist in how cells behave when division goes wrong. Sometimes a cell successfully copies its DNA but fails to split into two, leaving it with double the genetic material — a mistake linked to aging, cancer, and other major diseases. Researchers discovered that not all of these failures are equal.

mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... mix_items.title: Why restoring rivers isn't enough: New research shows fish are evolving in response to human-made rivers
mix_items.summary: This new international study is calling for a major rethink of how rivers are managed, arguing that fish are not just passive victims of environmental change but active participants in a feedback loop that can reshape entire river systems.

mix_items.title: A look at the SpaceX IPO by the numbers
mix_items.summary: Elon Musk is all about big numbers—millions, billions, even trillions—and there are plenty of them associated with SpaceX and Musk's plans to take the rocket maker public.

mix_items.title: Cosmic dawn fuel discovery unlocks early galaxy growth secrets
mix_items.summary: Astronomers have discovered a huge reservoir of cold molecular gas, the direct fuel for star formation, in REBELS-25, a massive, star-forming galaxy. The team, led from Leiden University, focused on REBELS-25, seen when the universe was only about 700 million years old, around 5% of its current age. The research is published in the journal Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society.

Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: CO₂ injection reveals hidden cement chemistry behind 13% stronger early strength
mix_items.summary: One September day, it started to snow inside MIT's Pierce Laboratory. Researchers depressurized a tank of liquid carbon dioxide (CO2), instantly freezing it and releasing solid flakes. These were blended into cement paste and pressed into disks roughly the size of a dime, each sealed with a thin layer of vegetable oil to keep water in and air out. The team trained lasers on each one, observing for the first time the transient chemical reaction that might explain why CO2-injected cement paste gains strength faster.

mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: New cavity control strategy improves performance of blue vertical-cavity surface-emitting lasers
mix_items.summary: GaN-based vertical-cavity surface-emitting lasers (VCSELs) are promising for displays, sensing and optical communication, but improving efficiency remains challenging. Researchers have now shown that "cavity tuning," which controls resonance wavelength, strongly affects laser performance. By analyzing variations across a VCSEL wafer, the team identified optimal mirror loss conditions and extracted device parameters. Their approach achieved 26.4% wall plug efficiency, offering guidance for next-generation high-efficiency visible-light semiconductor lasers.

mix_items.title: Third-grade impulses linked to lower academic achievement and education into adulthood
mix_items.summary: Can your behavior in third grade predict outcomes in high school and beyond? A new study, published in Developmental Psychology, says yes.

mix_items.title: Ancient DNA from Tuscan wells reveals origins of modern wine
mix_items.summary: Scientists analyzing 2,000-year-old grape seeds from ancient wells in Tuscany have mapped the most extensive genetic history of ancient grapevines recovered from a single site.

mix_items.title: Quantum friction causes light to slow down nanoworld movements
mix_items.summary: A research team in Bochum, Germany has unexpectedly found that light can slow down movements in the nanoworld. This is due to quantum friction, a phenomenon that has been poorly understood until now. The findings are published in the journal Nature.

mix_items.title: Personalized blood pressure control after thrombectomy boosts 90-day stroke recovery
mix_items.summary: Blood pressure management after thrombectomy for acute ischemic stroke may require a change in approach. The HOPE clinical trial—short for Hemodynamic Optimization of Cerebral Perfusion after Endovascular Therapy—led by the Sant Pau Research Institute (IR Sant Pau), has shown that adapting blood pressure targets to the degree of cerebral reperfusion significantly improves patients' functional recovery without increasing the risk of complications.

mix_items.title: The frontier of women's health care innovation
mix_items.summary: JMIR Publications has released a News and Perspectives story on technological innovations in women's health care. In "Bridging the Gender Gap in Health Care Innovation: The Evolution of FemTech," correspondent Jenny Castillo Cato covers how FemTech has expanded over the past decade to become a major force in women's health worldwide. The findings are published in the Journal of Medical Internet Research.

mix_items.title: Lead levels in children have dropped, yet children of color and children from low-wealth families are still exposed
mix_items.summary: Blood lead levels in young children in the United States declined in the last decade, but state data reveal that children of color and children from low-wealth families are still exposed to higher levels of lead, according to a study in the American Journal of Public Health. No level of lead in children's blood is safe.

mix_items.title: HERC2 gene's key role in rare neurodevelopmental syndrome deciphered
mix_items.summary: For years, it has been known that mutations in both copies of the HERC2 gene are associated with a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by global developmental delay, intellectual disability, features of the autism spectrum and movement disorders—characteristics very similar to those of Angelman syndrome, a better-known but equally rare condition. However, the exact function of the affected gene (HERC2) and the molecules with which it interacts remained unclear. Without understanding the biology underlying the syndrome, it was difficult to comprehend how it works and to devise therapeutic strategies to treat it.

mix_items.title: Nursing job turnover has nearly doubled since the pandemic
mix_items.summary: The COVID-19 pandemic did not push nurses out of hospitals or other care settings as feared, but nurses left their primary jobs at nearly double the rate from 2018 to 2022, finds a University of Michigan study. The study, which appeared in Medical Care, also found that the size of the nursing workforce grew from 3.27 million to 3.57 million during the same period.

mix_items.title: New antibody may boost KRAS-targeted lung cancer treatment after resistance emerges
mix_items.summary: An experimental antibody treatment that binds to a protein known as PCDH7 shrank tumors in preclinical models of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), including those resistant to a targeted therapy, a study led by UT Southwestern Medical Center researchers showed. The findings, published in Science Advances, could eventually lead to a new class of drugs to treat NSCLC and potentially other cancers.

mix_items.title: Math may transform how rural British Columbia hospitals manage life-saving blood donations
mix_items.summary: In medical emergencies, timely access to blood can mean the difference between life and death. Managing supply in rural communities is a major challenge, but Simon Fraser University researchers say part of the solution may be in the math.

mix_items.title: Researchers identify brain 'entrapment' patterns associated with depression
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai have identified distinctive patterns in how the brain transitions between activity states in people with depression, providing new insight into why depressive symptoms can feel persistent and difficult to overcome.

mix_items.title: Nuts hold key minerals, but digestion unlocks only part of them
mix_items.summary: The presence of minerals in oilseeds, such as Brazil nuts (Bertholletia excelsa) and cashews (Anacardium occidentale), does not guarantee that the body will use them. A study conducted at the Diadema campus of the Federal University of São Paulo (UNIFESP) in Brazil and published in the journal Química Nova evaluated the bioavailability of these nutrients. Bioavailability is defined as the fraction of the mineral actually released during simulated gastrointestinal digestion and potentially available for absorption.

mix_items.title: AI in nursing raises questions about safety, ethics, and human care
mix_items.summary: As artificial intelligence systems spread through hospitals and clinics, a growing debate is emerging over whether the technology will ultimately strengthen nursing care—or gradually replace parts of it.

mix_items.title: Early pregnancy exposure to heat and cold linked to differences in fetal size at 12 weeks
mix_items.summary: Moderate exposure to cold and heat during early pregnancy may affect fetal development as early as the first trimester, according to a new study led by the Barcelona Institute for Global Health (ISGlobal), a center supported by the "la Caixa" Foundation. The findings, based on two Dutch birth cohorts and published in the International Journal of Epidemiology, suggest that early gestational development may be sensitive to ambient temperature, with potential implications for birth outcomes and long-term health as climate conditions continue to change.

mix_items.title: Experts say ‘bare bones’ US laws are unfit to regulate nascent deep-sea mining industry
mix_items.summary: This is part 2 of a two-part series examining the U.S.’s efforts to begin deep-sea mining in federal waters. Part 2 examines the regulations that would govern the industry. Part 1 explored the process behind proposed lease sales in U.S. federal waters and reactions to those plans. The deep-sea mining industry could launch in the […]

mix_items.title: Colombia passes landmark cattle traceability law to combat illegal deforestation
mix_items.summary: Colombia passed a landmark law June 4 aimed at improving traceability of its cattle supply chain to ensure beef isn’t sourced from deforested land. The law hopes to enhance existing traceability systems and make it easier to identify when cattle have grazed in protected areas and forests that were illegally cleared for pasture. “This is […]

mix_items.title: Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga arrested at protest of national park construction
mix_items.summary: NAIROBI, Kenya (AP) — Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga said he was arrested Monday alongside other activists protesting planned construction inside Nairobi National Park. Police fired tear gas canisters at the protesters who were marching outside the park while carrying banners with messages denouncing land grabs. Maraga was detained and later released while staging […]

mix_items.title: Urban wildlife is changing from the inside out (commentary)
mix_items.summary: Cities are expanding faster than at any point in human history, and wildlife is adapting in remarkable ways. We often talk about visible changes like animals becoming bolder, shifting their diets, or altering their daily rhythms to avoid people. But there is a deeper transformation happening inside their bodies, one that conservation science has barely […]

mix_items.title: Scientists discover vast hidden structure beneath Antarctica’s ice
mix_items.summary: A giant fan-shaped network of hidden basins has been discovered beneath East Antarctica, revealing that several well-known subglacial features are actually part of one massive geological structure. The finding sheds new light on Antarctica’s ancient tectonic history and could help scientists better understand how the ice sheet behaves today.

mix_items.title: New laser heat treatment could stop blindness before it starts
mix_items.summary: A new experimental treatment could finally offer hope for millions of people with dry age-related macular degeneration — one of the leading causes of blindness in older adults. Researchers at Aalto University discovered a way to gently heat tissue at the back of the eye using near-infrared light, triggering the cells’ natural “cleanup and repair” systems before major damage occurs.

mix_items.title: This prehistoric fish may explain how animals first walked on Earth
mix_items.summary: Scientists have peered inside the skull of a 380-million-year-old Antarctic fish that was closely related to the first animals to walk on land, revealing surprising clues about how life began its move out of the water. Using advanced neutron imaging, researchers discovered that Koharalepis jarviki had features suited for living near the water’s surface, including openings in its skull that may have helped it gulp air and a light-sensitive organ linked to day-night rhythms.

mix_items.title: 100-million-year-old bug had crab-like claws unlike any known insect
mix_items.summary: Deep inside 100-million-year-old amber from Myanmar, scientists uncovered a bizarre ancient bug with clawed front legs that look more like a crab’s pincers than anything seen in modern insects. The discovery is so unusual that researchers say these crab-like “chelae” evolved independently in this lineage, making it only the fourth known example of such structures appearing in insects at all.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising twist in how cells behave when division goes wrong. Sometimes a cell successfully copies its DNA but fails to split into two, leaving it with double the genetic material — a mistake linked to aging, cancer, and other major diseases. Researchers discovered that not all of these failures are equal.

joke_ro.text: Am deschis frigiderul doar de trei ori. Progres emotional.

satire_ro.title: Cine este Securist S. Servicianu, propus de Tomac la Ministerul de Interne
